notes: Tree systems need working capital through establishment and moderate contingency.
Bee boxes and colonies need a modest startup reserve.
Livestock systems have steady feed and health cost pressure.
High-input drip systems need startup cash and repair allowance.
Aquaculture requires setup contingency and longer working-capital lockup.
Controlled systems carry higher utility and maintenance volatility.
Diversified systems need multi-enterprise working capital.
Indoor growing has recurring climate-control and sanitation sensitivity.
Protected cultivation requires startup reserve plus repair and pest buffers.
Nursery operations tie cash into media, trays, and mother stock before sales.
Broadacre and basic open-field systems have lower contingency but still need seasonal working capital.
Vegetables have faster cycles but higher harvest, packing, and price-volatility costs.
Perennial orchards require long establishment cash support before bearing.
Intercrops soften cashflow but orchard establishment still dominates startup costs.
Polyhouses need commissioning contingency and high recurring repair/sanitation buffers.
Bird mortality and feed-cost swings justify moderate working capital and contingency.
Dryland systems have lower setup costs but still need seasonal cash cover.
